name: The Underworld (Kur)
shortDescription: Kur is the Sumerian underworld, a dark and desolate realm beneath the earth where the spirits of the dead reside.
description: Kur, in Sumerian mythology, represents the underworld, a shadowy and often terrifying realm situated beneath the earth. It is a place of no return for the living, a land of dust and darkness where the spirits (gidim) of the deceased exist in a bleak and unenviable state. Unlike some other mythologies that envision an afterlife with rewards or punishments, Kur is generally depicted as a grim and monotonous existence. It is ruled by Ereshkigal, queen of the underworld, and her consort, Gugalanna. The journey to Kur is perilous and often involves crossing a river or traversing difficult terrain. The concept of Kur is significant because it reflects the Sumerian understanding of death and the afterlife, highlighting the finality and somber nature of mortality. It contrasts sharply with the vibrant and dynamic world of the living, emphasizing the separation between the two realms. The myth of Inanna's Descent to the Underworld provides a crucial insight into Kur, illustrating the strict laws and dangers within its borders. The idea of transformation, as seen in Inanna's ordeal, is also related to the archetype of death. The Kur also sometimes referred to as a primordial monster or dragon, the first-born of Nammu and An. This being was slain by the hero, Ninurta.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature
